volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: The Two Menaechmuses (Section 5.2.803 - Section 5.4.898)

sections.label: Section 5.2.803
sections.original: At ille suppilat mihi aurum et pallas ex arcis domo ,
sections.translation: But he purloins from me gold trinkets and mantles from out of the chests at home ; he plunders me , and secretly carries off my ornaments to harlots .

sections.label: Section 5.2.805
sections.original: Male facit , si istuc facit ; si non facit , tu male facis ,
sections.translation: He does wrong , if he does that ; if he does not do it , you do wrong in accusing him when innocent .

sections.label: Section 5.2.806b
sections.original: Quin etiam nunc habet pallam , pater ,
sections.translation: Why at this moment , even , he has got a mantle , father , and a bracelet , which he had carried off to her ; now , because I came to know of it , he brings them back .

sections.label: Section 5.2.808
sections.original: Iám ego ex hoc , ut factumst , scibo . íbo ad hominem atque adloquar .
sections.translation: I ’ ll know from himself , then , how it happened . I ’ ll go up to this man and accost him . ( Goes up to MENAECHMUS .) Tell me this , Menaechmus , what you two are disputing about , that I may know .

sections.label: Section 5.2.810
sections.original: quid tu tristis es ? quid illa autem irata abs te destitit ?
sections.translation: Why are you pensive ? And why does she in anger stand apart from you ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.811
sections.original: Quisquis es , quidquid tibi nomen est , senex , summum Iovem
sections.translation: Whoever you are , whatever is your name , old gentleman , I call to witness supreme Jove and the Deities —

sections.label: Section 5.2.812b
sections.original: Qua de re aut cuius rei rerum omnium ?
sections.translation: For what reason , or what matter of all matters ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.813
sections.original: Me neque isti male fecisse mulieri , quae me arguit
sections.translation: That I have neither done wrong to that woman , who is accusing me of having purloined this ( pointing to the mantle ) away from her at home ⋯ and which she solemnly swears that I did take away .

sections.label: Section 5.2.815_816
sections.original: sí ego intra aedis huius umquam , ubi habitat , penetravi pedem ,
sections.translation: If ever I set foot inside of her house where she lives , I wish that I may become the most wretched of all wretched men .

sections.label: Section 5.2.818
sections.original: Sanun es , qui istuc exoptes aut neges te umquam pedem
sections.translation: Are you in your senses to wish this , or to deny that you ever set foot in that house where you live , you downright madman ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.820
sections.original: Tun , senex , ais habitare med in illisce aedibus ?
sections.translation: Do you say , old gentleman , that I live in this house ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.821
sections.original: Tun negas ?
sections.translation: Do you deny it ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.821b
sections.original: Nego hercle vero .
sections.translation: By my faith , certainly do deny it .

sections.label: Section 5.2.821c
sections.original: Immo hercle † ludere negas ;
sections.translation: In your fun you are going too far in denying it ; unless you flitted elsewhere this last night . Step this way , please , daughter . ( To the WIFE .) What do you say ? Have you removed from this house ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.823b
sections.original: Quem in locum aut quam ob rem , obsecro ?
sections.translation: To what place , or for what reason , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.824
sections.original: Non edepol scio .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I don ’ t know .

sections.label: Section 5.2.824b
sections.original: Profecto ludit te hic . non tu tenes ?
sections.translation: He ’ s surely making fun of you .

sections.label: Section 5.2.825
sections.original: Iam vero , Menaechme , satis iocatu'siocatus es . nunc hanc rem gere .
sections.translation: Now , Menaechmus , you really have joked long enough ; now do seriously attend to this matter .

sections.label: Section 5.2.826
sections.original: Quaeso , quid mihi tecum est ? unde aut quis tu homo es ? ⋯
sections.translation: Prithee , what have I to do with you ? Whence or what person are you ? Is your mind right , or hers , in fact , who is an annoyance to me in every way ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.828
sections.original: Viden tu ílli oculos virere ? ut viridis exoritur colos
sections.translation: Don ’ t you see how his eyes sparkle ? How a green colour [*] is arising

sections.label: Section 5.2.829_830
sections.original: ex temporibus atque fronte , ut oculi scintillant , vide .
sections.translation: on his temples and his forehead ; look how his eyes do glisten ⋯

sections.label: Section 5.2.831
sections.original: Quid mihi meliust , quam quando illi me insanire praedicant ,
sections.translation: What is there better for me than , since they say I ’ m mad , to pretend that I am mad , that I may frighten them away from me ? ( He dances about .)

sections.label: Section 5.2.833
sections.original: Vt pandiculans oscitatur . quid nunc faciam , mi pater ?
sections.translation: How he yawns , as he stretches himself . What am I to do now , my father ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.834
sections.original: Concede huc , mea nata , ab istoc quam potest longissime .
sections.translation: Step this way , my daughter , as far as ever you can from him .

sections.label: Section 5.2.835
sections.original: Euhoe Bacche , Bromie , quo me in silvam venatum vocas ?
sections.translation: Evoë , Bacchus , ho ! Bromius [*] , in what forest dost thou invite me to the chase ? I hear thee , but I cannot get away from this spot , so much does this raving mad female cur watch me on the left side . And behind there is that other old he-goat , who many a time in his life has proved the destruction of an innocent fellow-citizen by his false testimony .

sections.label: Section 5.2.840
sections.original: Vae capiti tuo .
sections.translation: Woe to your head .

sections.label: Section 5.2.840b
sections.original: Ecce , Apollo mi ex oraclo ímperat ,
sections.translation: Lo ! by his oracle , Apollo bids me burn out her eyes with blazing torches .

sections.label: Section 5.2.842
sections.original: Perii , mi pater , minatur míhi oculos exurere .
sections.translation: I ’ m undone , my father ; he ’ s threatening to burn my eyes out .

sections.label: Section 5.2.843
sections.original: Ei mihi , insanire me aiunt , ultro cum ipsi insaniunt .
sections.translation: O me ! They say I ’ m mad , whereas they of themselves are mad .

sections.label: Section 5.2.844
sections.original: Filia , heus .
sections.translation: Hark you , daughter .

sections.label: Section 5.2.844b
sections.original: Quid est ? quid agimus ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ? What are we to do ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.844c
sections.original: Quid si ego huc servos cito ?
sections.translation: What if I call the servants out here ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.845
sections.original: ibo , abducam qui hunc hinc tollant et domi devinciant ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll go bring some to take him away hence , and bind him at home , before he makes any further disturbance .

sections.label: Section 5.2.846b
sections.original: Enim haereo ;
sections.translation: So now ; I think now if I don ’ t adopt some plan for myself , these people will be carrying me off home to their house . ( Aloud .) Dost thou forbid me to spare my fists at all upon her face , unless she does at once get out of my sight to utter and extreme perdition ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.850
sections.original: faciam quod iubes , Apollo .
sections.translation: I will do what thou dost bid me , Apollo .

sections.label: Section 5.2.850b
sections.original: Fuge domum , quantum potest ,
sections.translation: Away with you home as soon as possible , lest he should knock you down .

sections.label: Section 5.2.851b
sections.original: Fugio . amabo , ádserva istunc , mi pater ,
sections.translation: I ’ m off . Watch him , my father , I entreat you , that he mayn ’ t go anywhere hence . Am I not a wretched woman to hear these things ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.853
sections.original: Haud male , Apollo illanc amovi ; nunc hunc impurissimum ,
sections.translation: I ’ ve got rid of her not so badly . ( Aloud .) Now as for this most filthy , long-bearded , palsied Tithonus , who is said to have had Cygnus for his father [*] ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.855
sections.original: ita mihi imperas ut ego huius membra atque ossa atque artua
sections.translation: you bid me break in pieces his limbs , and bones , and members with that walking-stick which he himself is holding .

sections.label: Section 5.2.856b
sections.original: Dabitur malum ,
sections.translation: Punishment shall be inflicted if you touch me indeed , or if you come nearer to me .

sections.label: Section 5.2.858
sections.original: Faciam quod iubes ; securim capiam ancipitem , atque hunc senem
sections.translation: I will do what thou dost bid me ; I will take a two-edged axe , and I will hew this old fellow to his very bones , and I will chop his entrails into mincemeat .

sections.label: Section 5.2.860
sections.original: Enim vero illud praecavendumst , atque adcurandumst mihi ;
sections.translation: Why really against that must I take care and precaution . As he threatens , I ’ m quite in dread of him , lest he should do me some mischief .

sections.label: Section 5.2.862
sections.original: Multa mi imperas , Apollo : nunc equos iunctos iubes
sections.translation: Many things dost thou bid me do , Apollo . Now thou dost order me to take the yoked horses , unbroke and fierce , and to mount the chariot , that I may crush to pieces this aged , stinking , toothless lion .

sections.label: Section 5.2.865
sections.original: iam adstiti in currum , iam lora teneo , iam stimulus in manust .
sections.translation: Now have I mounted the chariot ; now do I hold the reins ; now is the whip in my hand . Speed onward , ye steeds , let the sound of your hoofs be heard ; in your swift course let the rapid pace of your feet [*] be redoubled .

sections.label: Section 5.2.868
sections.original: Mihin equis iunctis minare ?
sections.translation: Are you threatening me with your yoked steeds ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.868b
sections.original: Ecce , Apollo , denuo
sections.translation: Lo ! again , Apollo , thou dost bid me to make an onset against him who is standing here , and to murder him .

sections.label: Section 5.2.870
sections.original: sed quis hic est qui me capillo hínc de curru deripit ?
sections.translation: But what person is this that is tearing me hence by the hair down from the chariot ? He revokes thy commands and the decree of Apollo .

sections.label: Section 5.2.872
sections.original: Eu hércle morbum acrem ac durum ⋯ di vostram fidem .
sections.translation: Alas ! a severe and obstinate malady , i ’ faith . By our trust in you , ye Gods ⋯ even this person who is now mad , how well he was a little time since . All on a sudden has so great a distemper attacked him .

sections.label: Section 5.2.875
sections.original: ibo atque accersam medicum iam quantum potest .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll go now and fetch a physician as fast as I can .

sections.label: Section 5.3.876
sections.original: Iamne isti abierunt , quaeso , ex conspectu meo ,
sections.translation: Prithee , are these persons gone now out of my sight , who are compelling me by force , while in my wits , to be mad ? Why do I delay to be off to the ship , while I can in safety ? ⋯

sections.label: Section 5.3.879_880
sections.original: vosque omnis quaeso , si senex revenerit ,
sections.translation: And all of you ( to the SPECTATORS ) , if the old gentleman should return , I beg not to tell him , now , by what street I fled away hence .

sections.label: Section 5.3.882
sections.original: Lumbi sedendo , óculi spectando dolent ,
sections.translation: My bones ache with sitting , my eyes with watching , while waiting for the Doctor , till he returned from his business . At last the troublesome fellow has with difficulty got away from his patients .

sections.label: Section 5.3.885
sections.original: ait se óbligasse crus fractum Aesculapio ,
sections.translation: He says that he has set a broken leg for Aesculapius [*] , and an arm for Apollo . I ’ m now thinking whether I ’ m to say that I ’ m bringing a doctor or a carpenter [*] . But , see , here he comes .— Do get on with your ant ’ s pace .

sections.label: Section 5.4.889
sections.original: quid esse ílli morbi , dixeras ? narra , senex .
sections.translation: What did you say was his disorder ? Tell me , respected sir .

sections.label: Section 5.4.890
sections.original: num laruatust aut cerritus ? fac sciam .
sections.translation: Is he harassed by sprites [*] , or is he frenzied ? Let me know . Is it lethargy , or is it dropsy , that possesses him ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.892
sections.original: Quin ea te causa duco , ut id dicas mihi
sections.translation: Why , I ’ m bringing you for that reason , that you may tell me that , and make him convalescent .

sections.label: Section 5.4.893b
sections.original: Perfacile id quidemst .
sections.translation: That indeed is a very easy matter . Why , I shall heal innumerable times as many [*] in the day .

sections.label: Section 5.4.895
sections.original: Magna cum cura ego illum curari volo .
sections.translation: I wish him to be treated with great attention .

sections.label: Section 5.4.896
sections.original: Quin suspirabo plus sescenta ín die :
sections.translation: That he shall be healed , I promise that on my word ; so with great attention will I treat him for you .

sections.label: Section 5.4.898
sections.original: Atque eccum ipsum hominem . observemus , quam rem agat .
sections.translation: Why , see ! here ’ s the man himself .
